audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Deuteronomy / Devarim Moses-death and Torah-closing witness, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Nebo/Pisgah vision, promised land panorama, Moses' death at Divine mouth, unknown burial place, age and vigor, thirty-day mourning, Joshua's wisdom, Moses' unique prophetic intimacy, and Torah's closing signs-and-deeds witness.

verses.restored: Moses went up from plains of Moab to Mount Nebo, top of Pisgah, which is opposite Jericho. YHWH showed him all land: Gilead as far as Dan,
all Naphtali, land of Ephraim and Manasseh, all land of Judah as far as western sea,
the Negev and the plain, valley of Jericho, city of palms, as far as Zoar.
YHWH said to him, This is land I swore to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, saying, To your seed I will give it. I have caused you to see it with your eyes, but there you shall not cross.
Moses, servant of YHWH, died there in land of Moab, at mouth/command of YHWH.
He buried him in valley in land of Moab opposite Beth-peor, and no one knows his burial place to this day.
Moses was one hundred twenty years old when he died; his eye was not dim, and his vigor had not fled.
Children of Israel wept for Moses in plains of Moab thirty days; then days of weeping and mourning for Moses were completed.
Joshua son of Nun was full of spirit of wisdom, for Moses had laid his hands upon him; children of Israel listened to him and did as YHWH commanded Moses.
No prophet has risen again in Israel like Moses, whom YHWH knew face to face,
for all signs and wonders YHWH sent him to do in land of Egypt to Pharaoh and all his servants and all his land,
and for all strong hand and all great awe/terror that Moses did before eyes of all Israel.

translation_notes: The Torah ends outside the land, with vision rather than possession. Promise remains larger than the lifespan of its greatest leader.

Moses dies 'at the mouth of YHWH,' a phrase that later Jewish tradition beautifully receives as death by a Divine kiss; the restored layer preserves the Hebrew idiom without imposing one interpretation.
The unknown grave prevents possession of Moses' body and keeps the narrative from turning his burial place into a royal monument.
Verse 7 praises preserved sight and vigor at advanced age but must not imply that disability, frailty, dementia, or illness diminishes a person's dignity or spiritual completion.
Thirty days of communal mourning honor grief as a bounded public practice rather than weakness.
Joshua's wisdom is connected to Moses' laying on of hands, while the people remain responsible for listening and action.
The claim that no prophet arose like Moses is Torah's internal affirmation of his unique face-to-face relationship; it must not be used for interreligious contempt.
The final phrase 'before all Israel' closes Torah in communal sight and memory, not private esoteric possession.
safety_note: Do not use Deuteronomy 34 to shame aging, illness, disability, grief, or leaders who cannot complete every vision within one lifetime. Do not turn Moses' uniqueness into contempt for later prophets or religions. Center dignified death, communal mourning, non-dynastic succession, wisdom transmitted by touch, and a sacred work that continues beyond its founder.
